stewart_read: Randy confirms his wife is on the title. Holland acknowledges it ('Your wife? Yeah. She's on there. Alright. Great.') and moves immediately to the next verify item. The schema is explicit: co-title-holder must be included in the appointment. Holland captures the fact as data and discards it as a protocol trigger. By the time the appointment is booked at 12:41, the wife has never been mentioned again. This is a protocol violation, not a judgment call — the correct move was to say something like 'We'll want to make sure she's on the call with us too, since she's on the title. What time works for both of you?' Strategic read: there is no strategic reason to defer this — Holland simply didn't execute the protocol.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have planned to revisit spouse inclusion at button-up. The transcript shows no such revisit, so the strategic read does not hold.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask Holland: 'What did you do with the wife being on the title?' When Holland says 'I noted it,' ask: 'Where does she show up in the appointment?' The gap will be visible. Drill the spouse-inclusion pivot until it's automatic at the moment of disclosure, not deferred to button-up.

quote: need, like, 28. And that's gonna give me that's gonna give me a 150%. And do you have a battery?
stewart_read: Randy arrives with a specific panel count (28), an offset target (150%), and an unprompted battery requirement. This is a highly qualified, research-active customer — not a passive lead. The battery ask in particular is a cross-sell signal (context.ion_offerings_beyond_setter_call: Tesla Powerwall). Holland confirms battery availability in one sentence ('Yeah. We have batteries as well.') and moves on. The correct move was to anchor on the battery interest as a commitment signal: Randy has already decided he wants a battery, which means he's already mentally past 'if' and into 'how.' Holland walked past the strongest enthusiasm signal on the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that dwelling on the battery would extend the call unnecessarily. Given the subsequent scope creep into panel wattage, this restraint hypothesis is not supported — Holland was willing to go deep on technical content elsewhere.
coaching_implication: Show Holland the contrast: he spent two minutes debating panel wattage (closer-tier content) but gave the battery signal one sentence. The battery is a commitment anchor — 'You already know you want the battery, which means you've already thought this through more than most people I talk to. That's exactly what our specialist will build into the design.' That's the move. The wattage debate was the wrong place to spend the time.

quote: Do you happen to know the wattage of Dunham's panels?
stewart_read: Holland asks Randy about Dunham's panel wattage — opening a technical comparison conversation that belongs to the closer. Randy says he doesn't know offhand, and Holland immediately fills the gap by volunteering Ion's 440W spec and beginning to debate whether Randy's 28-panel estimate is accurate for Ion's equipment. The question itself is the scope-creep trigger: there is no setter-stage reason to know a competitor's panel wattage. The schema escape hatch was available: 'Our specialist will run the comparison against whatever design you got — that's exactly what the appointment is for.' Holland instead initiated a product-spec debate he was not positioned to win cleanly.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been trying to establish credibility by engaging Randy's technical knowledge. The effect was the opposite — Holland's hedged language ('I think that 28 panels might... I think 28 panels for us is high water wattage panels') signals uncertainty, not authority.
coaching_implication: This is the call's clearest scope-creep moment and the one most likely to recur with research-active customers. Coach Holland on the escape hatch: when a customer arrives with a competitor's quote and specific numbers, the setter's job is to make the comparison appointment irresistible, not to pre-run the comparison on the phone. 'You've already done the homework — let's make sure our specialist has everything they need to show you how we stack up' is the move.

quote: It's not gonna take too much longer, is it?
stewart_read: Randy signals time pressure mid-bill-collection. Holland's response ('No. No. This this is just the last thing that I need.') is adequate — it reassures and anchors to a single remaining task. However, the schema specifies a firm time anchor ('It's a 30-minute call — we'll get you in and out.') and Holland's response is softer than that: it addresses the immediate moment but doesn't anchor the total time commitment. Minor gap — Holland doesn't lose the call here — but the protocol calls for a firmer anchor than 'this is just the last thing.'
alternative_hypothesis: Holland's softer response may have been calibrated to Randy's tone — a firm scripted anchor might have felt abrupt given the conversational register. The response was functionally adequate; the gap is in precision, not outcome.
coaching_implication: Minor coaching point, best used as a contrast example alongside the bigger scope-creep and spouse-protocol gaps. Ask Holland: 'When Randy asked if this would take much longer, what did you tell him?' Then show the schema anchor. The lesson is that a firm time commitment ('just this one thing and we're done — two minutes') is more reassuring than a soft 'no, no.'

quote: Let's see. I have a then. And then I have a a secondary, Just Energy.
stewart_read: Randy discloses two utility providers but the primary utility name is never captured — the transcript shows Randy trailing off ('I have a then') before naming it, and Holland moves on without clarifying. verify.utility_company is a required item. Holland captures the secondary provider (Just Energy) but the primary utility — the one whose bill Holland is about to request — is never confirmed. This is a direct verify completion failure on a hard-required field.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have inferred the primary utility from the address (Joliet, Illinois → ComEd) and proceeded on that assumption. At 04:45 Holland references 'your ComEd bill' — suggesting this inference was made. The inference may be correct, but it was never confirmed with Randy, and the address itself was only partially corrected.
coaching_implication: Flag this in the 1-on-1 as a verify discipline gap. Holland inferred ComEd from geography rather than confirming it with Randy. Given that the address was also only partially corrected, building a bill-collection request on two unconfirmed assumptions compounds the risk. The fix is simple: 'And what's the name of your main electric provider?' before moving to bill collection.

quote: My mom passed away. We're having a funeral on May 2, so it's gonna have to be after that.
stewart_read: Randy discloses a bereavement mid-scheduling. Holland's response is 'Okay. After the second. Alright. I'm sorry to hear that, by the way.' — one sentence of acknowledgment followed by an immediate return to date-finding. The schema classifies this as a trust-gift moment: the customer is sharing something real and personal, and the response should acknowledge specifically before bridging. Holland's 'by the way' construction is particularly flat — it positions the condolence as an afterthought appended to the scheduling logic rather than a genuine human moment. The downstream effect is visible: Randy's energy at close ('or whatever') is the lowest point of the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that dwelling on the bereavement would feel intrusive or extend the call uncomfortably. Some customers prefer to keep business calls businesslike. However, Randy volunteered the information — he didn't have to explain why he was unavailable. That's a trust signal, not a request to move on.
coaching_implication: This is the most human moment on the call and the one most likely to affect show rate. Coach Holland on the difference between acknowledging and bridging: 'I'm so sorry — take all the time you need. When you're ready, we'll make sure this appointment works around everything you have going on.' That's two sentences. It doesn't extend the call materially, but it changes the emotional register of the booking. A customer who feels heard is more likely to show.

stewart_read: Holland captures the bill amount cleanly. Randy gives a precise number ($151.50), which signals he knows his bill and is engaged with the cost question. Holland's response is to move immediately to bill-collection logistics ('the only thing I'll need from you is just a copy of that kilowatt usage'). The schema is explicit: capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility. Holland captures and drops. The flip was available: Randy is already comparison-shopping (Sunrun quote, Dunham quote) and motivated by finding 'the best deal' — meaning the bill pain is real and the emotional lever is primed. Holland never pulls it.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that Randy's 'best deal' framing made a bill-pain flip unnecessary — Randy is already motivated. However, the flip's purpose is not to create motivation that doesn't exist; it's to anchor the utility as the adversary so the appointment feels like a solution, not a sales call. That anchor was never set.
coaching_implication: Show Holland the gap between capture and flip. The suggested move: 'A hundred and fifty-one dollars — that's what you're paying the utility every month whether solar works out or not. Our specialist's job is to show you whether we can beat that number. That's the whole point of the appointment.' No fabricated savings numbers, no promises — just reframing the bill as the cost of inaction. This is the floor's universal coaching gap per the schema.
